title: Planning Ahead: ACP, LPA and Wills
summary: Understand how advance care planning, a Lasting Power of Attorney and a Will work together.
keyPoints: ACP records values and care preferences for future healthcare discussions.
An LPA appoints trusted donees to act if mental capacity is lost.
A Will takes effect after death and directs how assets are distributed.

sections.heading: Why planning ahead matters
body: Planning ahead reduces uncertainty during illness or crisis. It gives families clearer guidance, makes decision-making less stressful and helps care teams understand what matters to the person receiving care.
Planning should happen while the person can participate meaningfully. It is not only for older adults or people who are already very unwell.

sections.heading: Advance Care Planning
body: Advance Care Planning, or ACP, is a process of discussing values, priorities and preferences for future healthcare. It can include what quality of life means to the person, who they trust to speak for them and what forms of treatment they may or may not want in certain situations.
ACP is not the same as refusing treatment. It helps future decisions reflect the person's values, even when circumstances change.

sections.heading: Lasting Power of Attorney
body: A Lasting Power of Attorney, or LPA, is a legal document that allows a person to appoint one or more donees to make personal welfare and/or property and affairs decisions if they lose mental capacity.
Choose donees carefully. Discuss expectations in advance, decide whether they should act jointly or separately, and ensure they understand the person's values and practical circumstances.

sections.heading: Making a Will
body: A Will states how a person's estate should be distributed after death and appoints an executor to carry out those instructions.
Review the Will after major life changes, such as marriage, divorce, the birth of a child, a significant change in assets or the death of an intended beneficiary or executor.

sections.heading: Conversation starters
body: Try: “I have been thinking about how we can make future decisions easier for everyone.”
Ask: “What matters most to you if your health worsens?” and “Who would you trust to speak on your behalf?”

sections.heading: Practical checklist
body: Locate identification, insurance, CPF, banking and property documents.
Record where the ACP, LPA and Will are kept and who has access.
Review documents and preferences periodically, especially after major health or family changes.

title: Caregiver Burnout: Recognise It Early
summary: Identify warning signs, reduce overload and make support part of the care plan.
keyPoints: Burnout is more than ordinary tiredness.
Support should be specific, scheduled and shared.
Urgent help is needed when safety is at risk.

sections.heading: What caregiver burnout can look like
body: Burnout may appear as persistent exhaustion, poor sleep, irritability, numbness, withdrawal, guilt, hopelessness or loss of interest in activities that previously mattered.
Physical signs can include headaches, muscle tension, changes in appetite and worsening of the caregiver's own health conditions.

sections.heading: Why it happens
body: Burnout is often driven by prolonged responsibility, uncertainty, disrupted sleep, financial pressure, family conflict, limited respite and the feeling that no one else fully understands the situation.
It is not a personal failure. It is a sign that the current care arrangement may be unsustainable.

sections.heading: A realistic action plan
body: List every recurring task. Mark what only you can do, what another family member can do, and what a service or professional could take over.
Make requests concrete: “Can you stay with Dad every Wednesday from 2 to 5 pm?” is easier to act on than “I need more help.”
Schedule breaks before reaching crisis point. Respite is part of safe care, not an optional reward.

sections.heading: Protecting your own health
body: Keep essential medical appointments, continue prescribed medicines and tell your doctor how caregiving is affecting sleep, mood, pain and daily functioning.
Maintain one identity outside caregiving through work, exercise, faith, friendships, hobbies or study.

sections.heading: When to seek urgent help
body: Seek immediate help if you may harm yourself or another person, if there is escalating violence or if caregiving tasks have become unsafe.
In a medical emergency, call 995. For immediate danger, call 999.

title: Home Safety and Care Equipment
summary: Reduce falls, choose equipment carefully and make the home easier to navigate.
keyPoints: Small environmental changes can prevent injury.
Equipment should be assessed for fit and safe use.
Caregiver safety matters during every transfer.

sections.heading: Start with the person's routine
body: Observe how the person moves from bed to toilet, bathes, dresses, eats and enters or leaves the home. The most useful modifications are those that address real daily difficulties.
Ask an occupational therapist or physiotherapist for assessment when mobility, balance, cognition or transfers are complex.

sections.heading: Bathroom safety
body: Use suitable non-slip surfaces, secure professionally fitted grab bars, adequate lighting and a stable shower chair if recommended.
Avoid relying on towel rails or suction grab bars for body weight unless they are specifically designed and installed for that purpose.

sections.heading: Bedroom and living areas
body: Clear walking routes, remove loose rugs and trailing cables, keep a light switch within reach and ensure commonly used items are easy to access.
The bed and chair should allow safer transfers. A height that is too low or too high can increase falls and caregiver strain.

sections.heading: Choosing equipment
body: Before buying a wheelchair, commode, hospital bed or transfer device, check the user's measurements, posture, strength, environment and caregiver capacity.
Rental may be useful for short-term or changing needs. Ask about maintenance, cleaning, replacement parts and training.

sections.heading: Protecting the caregiver
body: Do not lift alone when the person's weight, balance or behaviour makes the transfer unsafe.
Use transfer techniques taught by a trained professional and stop if pain, loss of balance or unexpected resistance occurs.

title: Palliative Care and Hospice Care
summary: Understand comfort-focused care and when to ask for additional support.
keyPoints: Palliative care can begin before the final stage of illness.
Hospice services support patients and families with life-limiting illness.
Asking early can improve symptom control and planning.

sections.heading: What palliative care means
body: Palliative care focuses on quality of life and relief of physical, psychological, social and spiritual distress. It can be provided alongside treatment intended to control disease or prolong life.
Support may address pain, breathlessness, nausea, fatigue, anxiety, family communication and future care planning.

sections.heading: What hospice care means
body: Hospice services generally support people with serious, life-limiting illness and their families. Care may be delivered at home, in inpatient hospices, day services, hospitals or through consultative teams depending on needs and eligibility.
Hospice care does not mean that care stops. The focus shifts towards comfort, dignity, meaningful time and support for the family.

sections.heading: When to ask for a referral
body: Ask when symptoms are difficult to control, hospital visits are increasing, the illness is progressing, family members are unsure what to expect or the person wishes to focus more on comfort.
Early referral allows time to build trust, discuss goals and prepare for emergencies.

sections.heading: Questions for the care team
body: What symptoms can your team help manage?
Who should we call after office hours?
Can care be provided at home?
What support is available for the caregiver and family?

title: Comfort During the Final Days
summary: Recognise common changes, focus on comfort and know when to call the care team.
keyPoints: Not every person experiences the same signs.
Eating and drinking often decrease naturally.
Uncontrolled symptoms should be reported promptly.

sections.heading: Possible changes
body: A person may sleep more, eat and drink less, become weaker, pass less urine, become confused or restless, and develop changes in breathing, circulation or skin temperature.
These signs can occur for different reasons. Contact the care team if you are uncertain or if changes are sudden or distressing.

sections.heading: Food and fluids
body: Offer small amounts gently when the person is awake and interested. Do not force food or fluids when swallowing is difficult or the person declines.
Mouth care, lip moisturiser and small sips when safe may provide more comfort than trying to meet usual intake.

sections.heading: Breathing and positioning
body: Repositioning, a calm environment, prescribed medicines and guidance from the care team may help with breathlessness or noisy breathing.
Speak normally and reassuringly. Hearing may remain even when the person does not respond.

sections.heading: When to call for help
body: Call the care team for uncontrolled pain, severe breathlessness, repeated vomiting, distress, bleeding, a fall, medicine concerns, new seizures or any change you do not know how to manage.
Follow the emergency plan provided by the treating or hospice team.

title: Caring for Children with Special Needs
summary: Support development, daily routines, transitions and caregiver wellbeing.
keyPoints: Build on strengths as well as needs.
Consistency across home, school and therapy helps.
Future planning should begin before a transition becomes urgent.

sections.heading: Understand the individual child
body: Children with the same diagnosis can have very different strengths, communication styles, sensory preferences and support needs.
Track what helps the child participate, learn, communicate and recover from stress. Share useful observations with the school and therapy team.

sections.heading: Daily routines
body: Use predictable sequences, visual schedules and simple step-by-step instructions where helpful.
Prepare for transitions with warnings, visual cues and limited choices. Reinforce effort and participation rather than expecting immediate independence.

sections.heading: Sensory needs
body: Notice whether the child seeks or avoids movement, noise, touch, light, smell or certain textures.
Create a calm space and use sensory activities recommended by relevant professionals. Avoid unsafe materials, choking hazards and unmonitored water play.

sections.heading: Working with professionals
body: Ask what goals are being targeted, how progress is measured and what can be practised safely at home.
Seek clarification when recommendations are difficult to carry out. A plan that fits family routines is more likely to be sustained.

sections.heading: Planning transitions
body: Discuss early intervention, school pathways, therapy, transport, financial planning, post-school options, employment and long-term care well before the transition date.
Include the young person's preferences and strengths wherever possible.

sections.heading: Caregiver wellbeing
body: Caregivers may experience chronic stress, isolation and uncertainty about the future. Peer networks, respite, counselling and practical help can reduce the burden.
Ask directly about caregiver support rather than waiting for services to raise it.
